_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
condition: Comminuted Fracture - Olecranon of Ulna / Fracture Head radius / Fracture intercondylar Humerus + olecranon osteotomy
source_files: Comminuted Fracture - Ulna, Head radius, intercondylar Humerus_Medco.pdf
Comminuted Fracture - Ulna, Head radius, intercondylar Humerus_PPD.CPD.pdf
min_doctor_qualification: Diploma in Orthopedics with 5 years' experience
clinical_indications: These injuries [comminuted fractures of Olecranon of Ulna] are frequently associated with concomitant elbow injuries, most frequently to the radial head and coronoid.

preauth.label: X-ray labelled with patient ID, date and side (Left/Right) - affected part

claim.label: Post procedure X-ray labelled with patient ID, date and side (Left/Right) - affected part

ppd_preauth.q: Was the clinical presentation, X ray report indicative of procedure?

common_queries: Clinical presentation or X-ray report not indicative of the procedure
Clinical notes detailing indication missing or incomplete
Clinical photograph of affected part missing
Pre-operative X-ray missing or not labelled with patient ID, date, and side (Left/Right)
Indoor case papers missing or incomplete
Procedure / operation notes missing or incomplete
Post-procedure X-ray missing or not labelled with patient ID, date, and side (Left/Right)
Invoice and barcode of implant missing
Discharge summary missing or lacking follow-up advice

clinical_key_pointers: The comminuted fractures of Olecranon of Ulna are relatively common injuries in adults.
These injuries are frequently associated with concomitant elbow injuries, most frequently to the radial head and coronoid.

Based on fracture comminution, displacement, and stability of the ulna-humeral joint, there are three types of fractures: Type I fractures are nondisplaced. Type II fractures are displaced at least 3mm Type III injuries the fracture is displaced, and the ulna-humeral joint is unstable.
